description: Dense morning bank at a mid-sized international airport. Six gates with strict size tiers, 12 flights compressed into a 6-hour window, 15-minute mandatory turnaround buffer. Four large-aircraft slots competing for 2 gates, mixed-size gate G4 creates cross-tier competition, and a simultaneous peak between 07:20–10:30 where 5–7 flights occupy gates concurrently. Expected chromatic number: 5.

pressure_points.label: Large gate bottleneck
pressure_points.description: F1 departs 07:30, F4 arrives 07:20 — only 10 minutes apart, below the 15-minute buffer. Both need G1 or G2, so they cannot share a gate. All four large flights (F1, F4, F8, F11) must be carefully sequenced across just two gates.

pressure_points.label: G4 cross-tier competition
pressure_points.description: G4 accepts both medium and small aircraft, making it a contested shared resource. Assigning a medium flight here frees G3 for another medium, but blocks small flights from using it — and small flights only have G5, G6, and G4 available.

pressure_points.label: Simultaneous peak 07:20–10:30
pressure_points.description: Between 07:20 and 10:30, up to 7 flights are at the gate simultaneously (F2, F4, F5, F6, F7, F8, F9). All 6 gates are occupied with zero slack. Any mis-sequencing in this window cascades.

pressure_points.label: Small aircraft squeeze
pressure_points.description: F3, F7, and F10 all need small-compatible gates (G4, G5, G6). F3 and F7 overlap (06:30–08:00 vs 08:10–09:30, within buffer), and F10 overlaps F7 — requiring all three small gates if G4 is not available.
